assignment_description:
Looking for Ants Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal

PREP: Read Teacher Tip

➜ INTRO
Today, you will look for ants!

➜ OBSERVE
Some good places to look are under small rocks, around logs, and in cracks. Use a stick to safely lift rocks. If you find ants, take some time to observe them. Use a magnifier if you have one. How many sections of the body can you see? From which do the legs come? Describe its antennae, jaws, and eyes. If you give it a drop of honey or a few crumbs, what does it do? If you find something else that you want to observe, you may take the time to do so. Spend about 10 minutes looking for ants.

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a nature journal to record what the student(s) noticed.

• Did you find ants? If so, describe their habitat. How does it provide food and shelter for them?
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
If students seem anxious or unsure about their nature journal, teachers can encourage them by modeling, showing them many different examples, journaling in tandem, or serving as a scribe.

★ TEACHER TIP
If students find ants, this is a great opportunity for an object lesson. Questions are embedded in the lesson to help, as needed. If preferred, teachers can instead refer to the Handbook of Nature Study or allow questions to flow naturally between student and teacher.

★ TEACHER NOTE
If you haven't ordered your caterpillars yet, don't forget!

assignment_description:
Insects and Plants
⍞ Materials: Monarch and Milkweed

PREP: Read Teacher Tip

➜ INTRO
Look at the picture on the cover. What kinds of butterflies have you noticed before? What did they look like? Where were they?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Monarch and Milkweed (whole book)

• The meadow where the flowers grow might be a good habitat for a butterfly, but they might also fly to other habitats to find flowers, as well. A 'biome' is all of the living things that live together in a community in neighboring habitats. Can you think of some different habitats in our biome, where we live, where butterflies might be able to find food and shelter?

• NATURE NOTEBOOK

Observing Caterpillars Activity

➜ OBSERVE

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a nature journal to record what the student(s) noticed.

• Do you recall in what stage of the life cycle the caterpillar is?

What does the butterfly do when it emerges from the chrysalis? If you have released them already, then spend your time noticing wildflowers. What colors and shapes do you notice? Do ants, butterflies, or other insects visit them? If so, what do the insects look like? What are the insects doing?

assignment_description:
Wildflower Watching Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal

PREP: Read Teacher Tip

➜ INTRO
Let's notice the wildflowers that are growing this season! Do you suppose that butterflies visit your wildflowers?

➜ OBSERVE
Spend about 10 minutes noticing which wildflowers are growing this season. What colors and shapes do you notice? How many petals do they have? How are the petals arranged? Do ants, butterflies, or other insects visit them? If so, what do the insects look like? How many different types or sizes of insects? What are the insects doing?

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a nature journal to record what the student(s) noticed.

• Do you notice anyone already familiar to you? Do you notice anyone new?

assignment_description:
Knowing a Tree Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal

PREP: Read Teacher Tip

➜ INTRO
Let's spend some time to get to know a particular tree. This will be your tree, so pick one that you can revisit often.

➜ OBSERVE
Choose a special tree near your home or school (i.e., one that you can visit regularly). Spend about 10 minutes getting to know your tree. You can even give it a special name. Does it have leaves on it right now, or have the leaves fallen? Look at them closely, so that you can see them with your eyes closed. What color are the leaves? Is their shape oval, heart-shaped, star-shaped, or something else? Are the edges smooth or jagged/ridged? With your eyes closed, touch the bark with your fingertips. What do you notice? Do you think you could tell this tree's bark from another without looking? If not, touch it again to get to know it better.

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a nature journal to record what the student(s) noticed.

• Tell about your tree.

• NATURE NOTEBOOK
Prompt for General Science in Outdoor Work Quick Link.
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
If students notice trees during their outdoor time, this could be a good opportunity for an object lesson. Questions are embedded in the lesson to help, as needed. If preferred, teachers can instead refer to the Handbook of Nature Study or allow questions to flow naturally between student and teacher.

assignment_description:
Meeting a Keystone Species
⍞ Materials: Desert Giant

PREP: Read Teacher Tip

➜ INTRO
Last term, you noticed that creatures in a community sometimes depend on each other, like butterflies and flowers. Have you ever noticed creatures other than butterflies and flowers that depend on each other? Look at the picture on the cover. This tree is a habitat for the birds, but so many other creatures depend on it, as well. So many creatures in its habitat depend on it that it is a "keystone species." Let's read and find out about that.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Desert Giant (whole book)

• Based on the story, what do you think is meant by "keystone species"? What creatures in your biome (or other creatures, if you are in the desert biome) seem to hold the whole community together?

➜ SUPPLEMENTAL
∞ Image Link: Wikipedia: Keystone

• NATURE NOTEBOOK
Prompt for General Science in Outdoor Work Quick Link.
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
Some students will grasp the 'big picture' that a keystone holds the larger structure together, but others might need help or might get stuck on the word itself. Use the link or draw a diagram, if needed.

assignment_description:
Observing a Seashell Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal, seashells, optional: a rock

➜ INTRO
In the marine/saltwater biome, oysters are a keystone species. Oysters eat algae, balance nutrient levels in the water, provide food, and build habitats for other creatures. Like oysters, many ocean creatures live inside shells that give them a unique way of life. It is amazing to think that the shells we find at the beach are the homes of other creatures! Let's notice some of these beautiful creature homes today!

➜ OBSERVE
Spend about 10 minutes today noticing the beauty and uniqueness of one or more seashells. What colors and patterns do you notice? What is the texture? Where do you suppose the animal resides?

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a nature journal to record what the student(s) noticed.

• Tell about what you recorded. Compare a seashell with a rock. How are they similar or different?

• NATURE NOTEBOOK
Prompt for General Science in Outdoor Work Quick Link.

Knowing a Tree Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal

PREP: Read Teacher Tip

➜ INTRO
Visit your tree again to learn something new about your friend.

➜ OBSERVE
Notice any twigs and/or fruit that you can collect. Study the twigs and/or fruit for about 10 minutes. What color is the twig? How does the bark compare to that of the tree? Is it straight or are there any bumps or shoots? If so, how are those bumps/shoots arranged?

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a nature journal to record what the student(s) noticed.

• Tell what you learned about your tree.
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP

Have you ever found a bird nest? What was it made of? How was it made? Where was it? What trees or other plants were nearby? These are all clues to who built it because each bird lives differently within its community.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Mama Built a Little Nest (whole book)

• Do you think anyone has had a nest in your tree? Why or why not?

assignment_description:
Knowing a Tree Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal

➜ INTRO
Changing our perspective (our view) can also help us notice more. Let's change our perspective and see what else we can notice about our tree.

➜ OBSERVE
Observe your tree from a distance and up close. How is your tree shaped? What is the land like around it? Do other plants or animals live near your tree? How do you think they help your tree? How do you suppose your tree helps them?

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a nature journal to record what the student(s) noticed.

• Tell what you learned about your tree.

Nest Investigation Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal

PREP: Read Teacher Tip

➜ INTRO
Spend about 10 minutes today looking for or studying a bird nest. If you don't find one, that's still a good observation! Note where you looked and what you did see during your search.

➜ OBSERVE
Since the songbirds are not nesting in the winter, any nests you find should be old and not in use, but observe them in place anyway. Another creature may reuse the nest or its material in the future. Discover as many clues as you can as to who built the nest. What was it made of? How was it made? Where was it? What trees or other plants were nearby?

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a nature journal to record what the student(s) noticed.

• If you found a nest, do you have an idea about who built it? You can ask a local naturalist or park ranger to confirm your guess - they love to help!
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
This could be a good opportunity for an object lesson on a bird's nest.

assignment_description:
Meeting a Unique Mammal
⍞ Materials: The Secret Life of the Skunk

PREP: Read Teacher Tip

➜ INTRO
Look at the picture on the cover. Have you ever seen a skunk? What would you do if you came across one on a walk? We don't often get to learn about skunks or any of the wild mammals in our biome up close, so let's read to find out more.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
The Secret Life of the Skunk (whole book)

• Have you ever smelled a skunk before? Take note of any skunky smell this term. When and where does it occur? How long does it last? Foxes smell very similar to skunks during mating season, but their season is a little earlier than skunks, and their odor doesn't tend to 'stick' quite as long.

• NATURE NOTEBOOK

assignment_description:
Mammal Investigation Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal

PREP: Read Teacher Tip

➜ INTRO
Wild mammals usually hide from humans, but we can learn about them by following their tracks. Spots with soft or wet soil are good for footprints, but if your soil is very dry, then you might still be able to find other signs, like scat or leftover food.

➜ OBSERVE
Spend about 10 minutes today looking for or studying animal tracks (or other sign). What is the size and shape? Are there claws visible? How many toes? Is there more than one animal? Are they the same or different? Which way are they pointing? How would you describe this habitat? What other plants and animals live in this biome?

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a nature journal to record what the student(s) noticed.

• Do you know who made any tracks or left any sign that you found? If not, what other clues might help? If so, you can ask a local naturalist or park ranger to confirm your guess - they love to help!
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
This could be a good opportunity for an object lesson on animal signs. Questions are embedded in the lesson to help, as needed. If preferred, teachers can instead refer to the Handbook of Nature Study or allow questions to flow naturally between student and teacher.

assignment_description:
Looking for Beetles Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal

PREP: Read Teacher Tip

➜ INTRO
Today, you will look for beetles, an insect that has a pair of hardened wings covering their flight wings.

➜ OBSERVE
Some good places to look are under small rocks, on plants, and in grass and leaf litter. Use a stick to safely lift rocks. If you find beetles, take some time to observe them. Use a magnifier if you have one. What parts of the body do you see? What colors are its hard shell? Describe its antennae, jaws, and eyes. If you give it a gentle nudge with a blade of grass or leaf, does it take off and let you see its wings? If you find someone else that you want to observe, you may take the time to do so. Spend about 10 minutes looking for beetles.

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a nature journal to record what the student(s) noticed.

• Did you find beetles? If so, describe their habitat. How does it provide food and shelter for them?

➜ SUPPLEMENTAL
∞ Video Link: Ladybug Wings (1:26)
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
This could be a good opportunity for an object lesson on a beetle or other insect. Questions are embedded in the lesson to help, as needed. If preferred, teachers can refer to the Handbook of Nature Study or allow questions to flow naturally between student and teacher.
